title: PAC Prediction Sets for Meta-Learning

reviews.summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.
reviews.questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.
reviews.limitations: The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
This paper builds on PAC prediction sets by extending it to few-shot meta-learning settings, where there is assumed access to a i.i.d. sequence of tasks. In few-shot meta-learning, the goal is to adapt to a new task with only a few training examples. In order to hope to do this successfully, the learner is also exposed to a collection of similar tasks (i.e., i.i.d. from some distribution over tasks) before being presented with the test task. From a confidence point of view, providing theoretically valid, non-vacuous confidence sets for few-shot learning is difficult. The work of [1] first proposed a method of also using the task distribution to help derive meaningful conformal prediction sets, that have marginal coverage guarantees over the draw of calibration tasks and test task. This paper adopts this setting, but casts it under the framework of PAC prediction sets, which offer high probability guarantees about the expected mis-coverage over new tasks, conditional on observed calibration tasks. On a high-level, PAC sets is to CP sets as Meta-PAC sets are to Meta-CP sets, and offer a different set of (desireable) performance guarantees. The paper empirically validates the effectiveness (and theoretical properties) of their algorithm on simulated few-shot meta-learning datasets (i.e., K-shot N-way image classification and relation classification tasks), as well as a realistic heart attack prediction task (where the "tasks" are stratified by year and state).

[1] Few-shot conformal prediction with auxiliary tasks. https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.08898.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
The paper is well-written (for the most part, see below) and the experiments are interesting. As the authors do well in pointing out, their PAC calibration framework offers several desireable guarantees that are not covered by the Meta-CP procedure. For example, Meta-PAC sets will be valid on average across all new tasks, rather than just on the (n + 1)th, marginalized over the draw of tasks {1, ..., n + 1}. Like standard PAC sets, this is nice, as it is often more practical to assume that the same fixed calibration set is reused for predictions across many future tasks. Therefore, even though prediction sets have been considered in a meta-learning setting before, this seems like a meaningful contribution.

Apart from a few specific comments/concerns, my main remark is on the readability of the presentation. Granted, I think the paper does an ok job considering the number of factors to consider simultaneously, but the notation is quite burdensome. It may be much easier to follow if a high-level, step-by-step description of what the authors want to accomplish and how they do it, is provided at the start of section 4---instead of jumping right into notation. For example, at a high-level, Meta-PAC sets are trying to find a single, shared threshold to use that 
1. On average over the random draw of calibration tasks that are used to choose this threshold...
2. and on average over the random choice of task that is used to evaluate this threshold...
3. and on average over predictions the model makes using this threshold on the new task...
4. The prediction set contains the correct label.

(Where we can replace "average" with high probability $1-\delta$, $1-\alpha$, $1-\epsilon$.)

I think this message is hinted at in various places in the manuscript, but it would be useful to say it plainly up front (obviously, not exactly in those words, but in simple, intuitive terms).

On to a few high-lighted strengths...

- The theoretical setting is appealing, and a reasonable and useful extension to Meta-CP (as stated above). 
- The calibration strategy seems correct and nice. At first I was concerned that computing PAC-thresholds for each of the calibration tasks was missing a factor of 1/N so that they are valid simultaneously, before applying the PAC method again on top of these values---as, with high probability, many $\hat{\tau}_{\epsilon, \alpha/2}(S_i, A_i)$ will _not_ be in $T_\epsilon(\theta_i, A_i)$ for large N. So we'd be calibrating the final threshold based on some "imposter" thresholds from a subset of tasks {1, ..., N}. However, my understanding is that since we only care about the output of a PAC method being i.i.d. (this is the r.v. O = PS-Binom(...) where O is valid w.p. 1 - alpha), not the output being valid itself, we do not actually need the PAC property to hold simulataneously over calibration tasks. Hence, the authors are able to avoid the 1/N factor in favor of two 1/2 factors (which is way more efficient). 
- Empirical results seem strong.

And a few weaknesses...

- It's kind of bothersome that a single threshold is used for _all_ downstream tasks; i.e., $\tau$ never gets updated, regardless of the size of the adaptation set A. In contrast, the Meta-CP method attempts to also estimate a data-dependent threshold (i.e., the adjusted quantile prediction). Therefore, when there is enough few-shot data, or significant variability across tasks, this could be better (in terms of being test-task conditionally). Here, the threshold never changes, regardless of whether the new task seems incredibly hard or incredibly easy.
- How tight are Clopper-Pearson based bounds when using small calibration task sizes? Assuming access to several hundred i.i.d. calibration tasks seems rather unrealistic, apart from very specific scenarios. The theory is technically solid, but a relatively straightforward combination of previous techniques (Meta-CP and PAC calibration). Thus I anticipate it to have only a relatively modest impact.

Questions:
- In the experiments it seems that you calibrate using tasks sampled from N choose k base calbration classes, and test on N choose k base (disjoint) test classes. Samples from the second are not i.i.d. w.r.t the second?
 - Lines 171-174 I can't understand. From what I can see, you simply want to estimate a value t s.t. $P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2, \delta}(S) \leq t) \geq 1 - \delta$. I'm not sure what $g$ is, and adding the dummy label also seems confusing.

Limitations:
The authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work. It might be worth to expand on the last sentence of the conclusion: namely that people should understand that this algorithm is not a cure-all for few-shot confidence estimation, but one that relies on several strong assumptions, and only holds on average across new tasks. That said, it is better than nothing!

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review  

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces a novel algorithm for constructing PAC prediction sets in meta-learning, extending PAC guarantees to settings where models adapt to new tasks with minimal labeled examples. The approach leverages calibration tasks to derive a shared threshold that ensures prediction sets satisfy a meta-PAC guarantee—high-probability coverage over future tasks—addressing a key limitation of prior methods like Meta-CP that offer only marginal guarantees. The authors validate their method rigorously across simulated few-shot tasks (K-shot N-way classification on mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C, and FewRel) and a realistic heart attack prediction task (CDC Heart Dataset), demonstrating smaller prediction sets while meeting theoretical error constraints.  

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**  
*Strengths*:  
- **Theoretical Novelty**: The work is highly original, introducing the first meta-PAC guarantee (Theorem 1) for task-conditional error control, a clear advance over prior conformal prediction methods. The calibration strategy efficiently combines PAC-Bayesian techniques without requiring simultaneous validity over calibration tasks, avoiding costly \(1/N\) factors.  
- **Empirical Rigor**: Experiments across diverse domains (vision, NLP, healthcare) robustly validate the approach. Results show consistent satisfaction of the meta-PAC guarantee and reduced prediction set sizes compared to baselines like naive PAC pooling and Meta-CP.  
- **Practical Significance**: The framework addresses a critical need for uncertainty quantification in safety-critical meta-learning applications (e.g., healthcare), particularly where fixed calibration sets are reused across tasks.  

*Weaknesses*:  
- **Readability and Notation**: The presentation is dense, with burdensome notation obscuring intuition. A high-level, step-by-step overview of Meta-PAC (e.g., clarifying the interplay of thresholds across task draws and predictions) should precede Section 4’s technical details. Figures 1–3 help but could better demystify the conditioning structure.  
- **Inflexible Thresholding**: A key limitation is the use of a *single fixed threshold* for all downstream tasks, regardless of task difficulty or adaptation set size. This contrasts with Meta-CP’s data-dependent quantile adjustment, which may better handle task variability.  
- **Data Efficiency and Scope**: Clopper-Pearson bounds may be loose with small calibration tasks (\(n\)), and the requirement for hundreds of i.i.d. calibration tasks is unrealistic in many scenarios. While technically sound, the method combines existing techniques (Meta-CP + PAC calibration), suggesting modest impact. Broader empirical sensitivity analysis (e.g., to \(N\), \(n\), or distribution shifts) would strengthen claims.  

**Questions:**  
1. **Algorithmic Design**: Why split \(\alpha\) into \(\alpha/2\) for both calibration steps (Algorithm 1)? Provide theoretical/empirical justification for this choice and its impact on statistical power.  
2. **Threshold Estimation (Lines 171–174)**: Clarify the role of the dummy label and the function \(g\) in constructing \(S'\). The current description is confusing—how does this align with the goal of estimating \(\tau\) such that \(P(\hat{\tau}_{\alpha/2,\delta}(S) \leq \tau) \geq 1 - \delta\)?  
3. **Task Exchangeability**: In experiments, calibration and test tasks are drawn from disjoint class sets. How does this align with i.i.d. assumptions? How would performance degrade under mild task distribution shifts?  
4. **Computational Costs**: What is the runtime overhead versus baselines (e.g., Meta-CP)? How does scaling with \(N\) (calibration tasks) and \(n\) (samples per task) affect practicality?  
5. **Reproducibility**: Detail hyperparameters (e.g., architectures, learning rates) and computational resources for all experiments.  

**Limitations:**  
The authors adequately address data requirements and potential misuse (Appendix F), but several points merit expansion:  
- **Societal Impacts**: Ethical risks (e.g., healthcare misapplication due to overreliance on guarantees) should be discussed in the main text, not just the appendix. Explicit failure cases where i.i.d. assumptions break could prevent unsafe deployments.  
- **Scope of Guarantees**: Emphasize that this is not a universal solution—it relies on strong task-exchangeability assumptions and offers *average-case* coverage, not per-task guarantees. Performance may degrade with task heterogeneity or label imbalance (e.g., CDC Heart’s class skew).  
- **Broader Baseline Comparison**: While conformal methods are covered, comparison to Bayesian meta-learning baselines would contextualize uncertainty quantification tradeoffs.  

**Recommendation:**  
This paper makes a **meaningful contribution** by extending PAC guarantees to meta-learning with theoretical rigor and thorough empirical validation. While the presentation can be streamlined and limitations around threshold flexibility/data efficiency remain, the core ideas are technically sound and address an important gap. The work is a **solid, incremental advance** suitable for publication.  

**Rating:**  
**6: Weak Accept** — Technically solid, moderate-impact paper with no major flaws in evaluation or ethics. Strengths in novelty and validation outweigh weaknesses in exposition and scope.

reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

This paper proposes a PAC prediction set algorithm for meta learning. The goal is to adapt the predictor to new unseen tasks with a few labeled training examples. Experiments on four datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed methods with  PAC guarantee while having smaller prediction set size.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:


* The paper is well-written.
* The proposed method is reasonable with theoretical analysis.
* Experiments on various domain and datasets are compared.


Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

Questions:
Please refer to weaknesss above.

Limitations:
Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review  

**Summary:**  
This paper proposes a novel PAC prediction set algorithm for meta-learning, enabling uncertainty quantification with theoretical guarantees when adapting predictors to new tasks with minimal labeled examples. The core contribution is a **meta-PAC guarantee** ensuring correctness holds with high probability over future tasks, a significant advancement beyond prior methods offering only average guarantees. The approach leverages calibration tasks to learn prediction set parameters without additional test-time calibration. Experiments across four diverse domains—computer vision (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C), NLP (FewRel), and healthcare (CDC Heart Dataset)—validate the method’s effectiveness in satisfying theoretical guarantees while reducing prediction set sizes compared to baselines like naive PAC pooling and meta conformal prediction.  

### Strengths  
* **Technical Soundness:** The proposed method is well-motivated and supported by a rigorous theoretical foundation, including a proven meta-PAC guarantee (Theorem 1).  
* **Experimental Robustness:** Comprehensive evaluations across varied domains and datasets strengthen claims of generality. The inclusion of healthcare data (CDC Heart) highlights practical applicability.  
* **Originality:** This work is the first to provide task-conditional PAC guarantees for meta-learning prediction sets, addressing a critical gap in safety-critical applications.  
* **Clarity:** The paper is well-structured overall, with effective graphical abstractions (e.g., Figure 2 illustrating guarantees).  

### Weaknesses  
* **Methodological Scope:** The approach is currently limited to metric-based meta-learning (e.g., prototypical networks). Extending it to optimization-based methods (e.g., MAML) would broaden impact.  
* **Calibration Rigidity:** The meta prediction set parameters remain fixed after adaptation; adapting these parameters during task-specific fine-tuning could improve performance.  
* **Experimental Breadth:** For mini-ImageNet, only 5-way classification is evaluated. Testing on more challenging settings (e.g., 10-way or 20-way) would better demonstrate scalability.  
* **Technical Justification:** Key design choices (e.g., splitting α/2 for calibration steps in Algorithm 1) lack thorough theoretical or empirical justification.  

### Questions  
1. **Algorithm Design:** Why was α/2 chosen for both calibration steps in Algorithm 1? Does this impact statistical power or generalizability?  
2. **Computational Efficiency:** How does the computational overhead scale with the number of calibration tasks (*N*) and examples (*n*)? How does runtime compare to baselines like Meta-CP?  
3. **Robustness:** How does performance degrade under violations of task exchangeability (e.g., distribution shifts or adversarial tasks)?  
4. **Hyperparameter Sensitivity:** Can you clarify the impact of hyperparameters (α, δ) on prediction set size (Table 1) and guarantee satisfaction?  
5. **Data Efficiency:** For CDC Heart’s class imbalance, how were task distributions defined? How does adaptation handle classes unseen during training?  

### Limitations  
The authors acknowledge limitations regarding calibration data requirements and potential societal impacts (e.g., misuse in healthcare due to overreliance on guarantees). However, the following could be expanded:  
* **Practical Deployment:** Explicit discussion of failure cases where distributional assumptions break (e.g., non-i.i.d. tasks) is needed.  
* **Societal Impacts:** Ethical considerations (e.g., calibration data biases propagating to predictions) should be moved from the appendix to the main text.  
* **Resource Constraints:** Computational costs for large-*N* experiments and sensitivity to hardware resources warrant further detail.  

### Decision  
**Rating: 6 (Weak Accept)**  
This paper presents a **technically solid contribution** with a novel theoretical framework for task-conditional uncertainty guarantees in meta-learning. The multi-domain validation and rigorous proofs support its moderate-to-high impact, particularly for safety-critical applications. However, the **narrow scope** (limited to metric-based methods), **insufficient justification for key design choices**, and **lack of scalability tests** (e.g., higher-way classification) preclude a higher rating. Addressing the raised questions in revision would strengthen the work.  

---  
**Hybrid Integration Notes:**  
- **Summary:** Combines the main review’s brevity with alternative reviews’ emphasis on multi-domain validation and theoretical novelty.  
- **Strengths:** Retains the main review’s core strengths while incorporating the alternatives’ focus on originality, theoretical rigor, and practical significance.  
- **Weaknesses:** Augments the main review’s critiques with alternative reviews’ concerns about α/2 justification and computational overhead.  
- **Questions:** Merges the main review’s methodological questions with alternatives’ technical (e.g., hyperparameter sensitivity) and societal (e.g., bias propagation) queries.  
- **Limitations:** Preserves the main review’s acknowledgment of societal impacts while integrating alternatives’ requests for expanded discussion of distributional robustness.  
- **Decision:** Maintains the "Weak Accept" rating while reflecting the alternatives’ enthusiasm for theoretical contributions and the main review’s reservations about scope.

Weakness:



* For reusing the same meta prediction set parameter after adaptation, would be better if the meta predictions set parameters are also adapted?
* The current methods seem only applies on metric-based meta-learning methods, e.g., prototypical network. It would be better if the methods could be also applies on optimization-based methods, such as model-agnostic meta learning (MAML).
* For mini-ImageNet datasets, only 5-way classification problem is compared. It would be better if the authors can compare on more challenging, 10-way or 20-way classification.

Questions:
Please refer to weaknesss above.

Limitations:
Yes, the authors have addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work.

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.

Summary:
This paper considers the notions and algorithms for generating a prediction set under the meta learning setting that satisfies PAC guarantees. Since there are multiple distributions involved in meta learning setting, the considered PAC notion for meta learning is different from existing notion designed for conventional machine learning setting, such as [3]. Furthermore, the meta learning setting is also different from covariate shift case considered in [6]. For example, adaptation data sets are available for a trained model to adapt these associated distributions without estimation of importance weights. Therefore, meta-PAC (mPAC) notion is proposed (Definition 3), which uses an augmented calibration set from N distributions, i.e., $p_{\theta_1}, …, p_{\theta_N}$ to generate $\varepsilon$-correct prediction set for a testing distribution $p_\theta$ whp $1-\delta$. Algorithm 1 is proposed to determine the parameter $\tau$ of prediction set, which uses a two-step strategy to first determine ($\varepsilon, \alpha/2$)-PAC estimator/threshold on each of N distributions, and then use these thresholds (with a label 1 attached to each threshold) to construct a new sample of size N to determine a threshold with ($\alpha/2, \delta$)-PAC. Theorem 1 shows the guarantee of this algorithm for ($\varepsilon, \alpha, \delta$)-mPAC.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Originality:
This paper generalizes PAC notation of conformal prediction from previous studies to mPAC in meta learning case, where multiple distributions are present, augmented calibration set is available. Algorithm is proposed to guarantee mPAC built on the existing algorithm designed for PAC prediction. 

Quality:
This paper is technically sound and theoretical analysis is clear.

Clarity:
This paper is easy to follow.

Significance: 
This paper provides insights to conformal prediction in meta learning setting.

Questions:
Algorithm 2 involves grid search for $\tau$. Could the authors elaborate a bit more on its computational cost/efficiency in analysis or experiment (e.g., the Granularity for searching)? Would there be any possible way to accelerate such grid search?

Limitations:
I think the authors adequately addressed the limitations and potential negative societal impact of their work

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review  

### Summary  
This paper introduces novel algorithms for generating prediction sets under meta-learning settings that satisfy Probably Approximately Correct (PAC) guarantees. The work proposes a distinct meta-PAC (mPAC) notion (Definition 3) to address the multi-distribution nature of meta-learning, differentiating it from conventional PAC frameworks [3] and covariate shift settings [6]. Algorithm 1 determines the prediction set parameter τ via a two-step calibration strategy: first computing (ε, α/2)-PAC thresholds across N distributions and then constructing a new sample (with dummy label 1) to derive a (α/2, δ)-PAC threshold. Theorem 1 formally establishes the (ε, α, δ)-mPAC guarantee. The approach is validated empirically across diverse domains—computer vision (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C), NLP (FewRel), and healthcare (CDC Heart)—demonstrating adherence to theoretical guarantees while achieving smaller prediction sets compared to baselines like naive PAC pooling and meta conformal prediction.  

### Strengths and Weaknesses  
**Originality:** The work is highly original, extending PAC guarantees to meta-learning with a novel three-parameter mPAC formulation. It clearly differentiates from prior work by providing *task-conditional* guarantees (unlike average-case baselines) and leverages meta-learned score functions without test-task calibration. Related work is well-cited.  

**Quality:** The paper is technically sound, with rigorous proofs (Theorem 1) and thorough experiments validating claims across multiple domains. The theoretical analysis is clear, and methods are appropriate. Limitations (e.g., calibration data requirements) are honestly addressed. However, the justification for using α/2 in both calibration steps (Algorithm 1) could be expanded.  

**Clarity:** The paper is generally well-structured and easy to follow. Figures 2 and 3 effectively illustrate guarantees and results, though Figure 1’s notation could be simplified. Section 4.2’s algorithm description might benefit from greater intuition.  

**Significance:** The work provides valuable insights for conformal prediction in meta-learning, particularly for safety-critical applications (e.g., healthcare, autonomous systems). It offers demonstrable improvements in error control and set size reduction, representing a moderate-to-high impact advance.  

### Questions  
1. **Algorithm 2’s Computational Cost:** The grid search for τ could incur overhead. Could the authors elaborate on granularity choices, computational efficiency (e.g., runtime scaling with N/n), and potential acceleration strategies?  
2. **α/2 Split Justification:** Why was α/2 chosen for both calibration steps? Does this impact statistical power or admit optimization?  
3. **Robustness to Assumptions:** How does performance degrade under (a) mild violations of task exchangeability or (b) distribution shifts beyond i.i.d. tasks?  
4. **Practical Efficiency:** What computational overhead does Meta-PS introduce versus baselines (e.g., Meta-CP)? How do minimum n/N requirements affect guarantees?  
5. **CDC Heart Adaptation:** How were class imbalances handled during task adaptation, especially for unseen classes?  

### Limitations  
The authors adequately address calibration data needs and potential societal impacts (Appendix F). However, the discussion could be strengthened by:  
1) Explicit analysis of failure cases where distributional assumptions break (e.g., adversarial tasks),  
2) Expanded ethical considerations for healthcare deployment (e.g., bias propagation from calibration data), and  
3) Sensitivity analysis of hyperparameters (α, δ) on prediction set size.  

### Rating  
**6: Weak Accept**  
Technically solid with clear theoretical contributions and thorough empirical validation across domains. While the novelty and practical utility are notable (supporting higher significance than initially noted), the moderate-to-high impact aligns with a "Weak Accept." Minor concerns regarding computational efficiency, robustness, and expositional clarity do not undermine the core contributions. Reproducibility and ethical considerations are adequately addressed.

reviews.summary:
POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.
reviews.questions: What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?
reviews.limitations: I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

POST DISCUSSION:

The authors addressed most of my issues about readability and the attribution problem, so I have increased my score to a weak accept.

My final assessment is as follows:
I believe the proofs are correct, and the related work is correct.
I think the paper is the first to do a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which has some value. Although this guarantee is a straightforward extension of existing results, perhaps somebody might be interested in it, and I do not believe it has appeared before.

I also believe it contributes by being the second paper to use training-conditional conformal for meta-learning. It is slightly different from the existing work by Adam Fisch et al. in that it also conditions on the tasks. Maybe somebody will find that useful!

_____________
This paper introduces a form of distribution-free uncertainty quantification guarantee into meta-learning and proposes an algorithm to achieve it. The guarantee is a triply-nested one, where the innermost integral is an expectation on a 0-1 loss. This expectation is nested within two other probabilities, conditioned on the calibration set and the meta learned parameters.  The algorithm boils down to two applications of binomial concentration. There are then extensive experiments on several meta learning tasks in vision, language, and medicine.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: I the technical results are clearly correct. The experiments are comprehensive.

Weaknesses:

(1) The guarantee provided in this paper is so complicated to interpret that I worry it will never be used.  It is already difficult enough to interpret a tolerance interval, and adding an extra parameter incurs such a high cognitive cost for the user that one may never know how to set these parameters.

(2) The paper is difficult to understand, as the writing is dense and exceedingly complicated. For example, take Figure 2. It includes a lot of extraneous information, the colors are missing, and certain aspects of the plot are not explained. What do the orientations of the stripes mean, for example? What does the box with “N” mean? Another part that is hard to read is 4.1.  There is just too much going on and I’m not sure anybody except for a very small community working on these PAC type guarantees will understand it.

(3) The technical result is not too interesting and the domain area is not novel.  The meta conformal prediction paper already provides a guarantee in this problem domain that works well, although it is marginal on the meta learned parameters. The fact that we can apply binomial concentration twice as opposed to once in order to condition again on those variables is basically an extension of existing results. Having said that, I think it is interesting and clever that we can chain the concentration argument twice. Nice work on the algorithm. 

(4) I believe there is a major attribution issue throughout the work. The idea of training-conditional prediction sets was first proposed by Vovk [21]. Rebranding it as PAC prediction sets and citing both works as the source of the idea is wrong. The idea comes from Vovk and his paper should get all of the credit for that.  Please remove all instances of the following attribution: “PAC prediction sets [3]”. The same is true of the so-called PS-Binom algorithm, which is essentially the same as that of Vovk in Proposition 2b of [21].  The paper [3] can be credited for applying [21] in a deep learning setting, but [21] should be cited as the sole inventor of this idea, in this and any future work. I also take some issue with the rebranding as PAC prediction sets as opposed to training-conditional validity, as they are exactly equivalent and the latter terminology came first. I will argue for rejection of the paper unless these attributions are amended.

Questions:
What does it mean for tasks to be “iid” in the real world, outside of synthetic setups? What are some realistic settings where we might expect the guarantee provided from the authors to actually hold?

Limitations:
I believe the limitations have been addressed. (I do not believe there are any serious ethical limitations of this paper in the first place, and ethics review should certainly not get in the way of the publication of this paper.)

Rating:
6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review  

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces a novel algorithm for constructing PAC prediction sets in meta-learning, providing uncertainty quantification with theoretical guarantees that hold with high probability over future tasks. The approach leverages meta-learned score functions and constructs prediction sets via a two-step calibration process, satisfying a three-parameter meta-PAC guarantee. While the technical results are clearly correct and the experiments are comprehensive, the guarantee’s complexity raises concerns about interpretability and usability. The paper is the first to propose a two-stage training-conditional guarantee, which—though a straightforward extension of existing results—adds value as a clever application of chained binomial concentration. It also contributes as the second work to apply training-conditional conformal prediction to meta-learning, differentiating itself by conditioning on tasks. However, significant attribution issues must be addressed to avoid misrepresenting prior work.  

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**  
**Strengths:**  
- **Technical Soundness:** The proofs (Theorem 1) are rigorous, and experiments across vision (mini-ImageNet, CIFAR10-C), language (FewRel), and medical domains (CDC Heart) robustly validate the claims.  
- **Algorithmic Contribution:** The two-step calibration process is a clever extension of binomial concentration, enabling task-specific guarantees.  
- **Significance:** Addresses a critical need for uncertainty quantification in safety-critical meta-learning applications (e.g., healthcare).  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Interpretability and Usability:** The triply-nested guarantee is prohibitively complex. Users may struggle to set parameters (δ, α, ε) or understand trade-offs, especially outside synthetic i.i.d. task settings. The cognitive cost risks limiting adoption (*Main Review, Alt4*).  
2. **Clarity Issues:** The writing is dense, particularly in Section 4.1 and Figure 2 (e.g., unexplained stripe orientations, "N" annotation). While figures abstractly contrast guarantees, they lack intuitive explanations for non-specialists (*Main Review, Alt3*).  
3. **Attribution Problems:** Training-conditional guarantees originate solely from Vovk [21], not [3]. The rebranding as "PAC prediction sets" and misattribution of PS-Binom (equivalent to Vovk’s Prop. 2b) must be corrected. [3] should only be credited for deep learning applications (*Main Review*).  
4. **Limited Novelty:** The core technique chains two binomial concentration steps—an elegant but incremental extension of existing conformal methods. While it provides task-conditional guarantees (unlike prior meta-conformal work), the domain is not groundbreaking (*Main Review, Alt1*).  
5. **Underdeveloped Elements:** Justification for splitting α/2 in Algorithm 1 is lacking (*Alt3*), and computational overhead versus baselines (e.g., Meta-CP) is unaddressed (*Alt4*).  

**Questions:**  
1. **Practical Assumptions:** What realistic settings (beyond synthetic) justify i.i.d. tasks? How does performance degrade under distribution shifts (*Main Review, Alt4*)?  
2. **Algorithm Design:** Why α/2 in both calibration steps? Does this impact statistical power or set sizes (*Alt1, Alt3*)?  
3. **Scalability:** How do computational costs scale with calibration tasks (N) and examples (n) (*Alt2*)?  
4. **Robustness:** Could the method handle non-scalar parameterizations or unbalanced tasks (e.g., CDC Heart’s class imbalance) (*Alt1, Alt4*)?  

**Limitations:**  
- **Data Efficiency:** Requires sufficient calibration tasks (N) and samples (n) for guarantees, which may limit low-data applications (*Alt3*).  
- **Societal Impacts:** Potential for misuse if guarantees are overtrusted in non-i.i.d. settings (e.g., healthcare). Appendix F should be expanded in the main text to discuss failure cases and calibration data biases (*Alt1, Alt4*).  
- **Scope:** Sensitivity to hyperparameters (α, δ) and computational costs of calibration warrant explicit analysis (*Alt4*).  

**Decision:**  
**Rating: 6 (Weak Accept)**  
This technically solid paper extends PAC guarantees to meta-learning via a novel two-stage calibration approach, validated across diverse domains. While the guarantee’s complexity and attribution inaccuracies are significant concerns, the authors’ corrections to attribution (particularly crediting Vovk [21]) and the method’s empirical performance justify weak acceptance. The work provides a moderate advance for safety-critical applications but requires clearer exposition and broader accessibility to achieve high impact.  

---  
**Changes from Main Review:**  
- Integrated alternative reviews’ emphasis on *significance for safety-critical applications* and *task-specific guarantees*.  
- Added weaknesses regarding *computational overhead* (Alt2, Alt4) and *α/2 justification* (Alt3).  
- Expanded limitations to include *societal impacts* (Alt1) and *hyperparameter sensitivity* (Alt4).  
- Incorporated questions on *distribution shifts* (Alt4) and *scalability* (Alt2).  
- Maintained the main review’s core concerns about *attribution*, *complexity*, and *novelty*, while preserving the "weak accept" rating.

reviews.summary: The paper proposed a method for estimating the prediction sets in the meta-test stage from the estimated prediction sets gathered from the meta-train adaptation and calibration phases. The whole process is carefully designed to satisfy the PAC setting in provide a theoretical guarantee for generating the meta-test prediction sets without calibration. The proposed method is tested on four different datasets across three application domains with competitive results.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The proposed method is guaranteed by PAC bound. 

Weaknesses:
1. The paper is not presented in a good way. The notation is very confusing and misleading in general.
2. A big picture is missing. For example, we know that f is a pre-trained meta-model. But in Figure 1 this information is missing which may confuse the meta-learning people.
reviews.questions:
1. In terms of evaluation, I think both predictions set error and prediction set size are considered as the measurements. Among the experiment settings, Meta-PS shows relatively low set error but in some cases set size is larger than others. I think the idea case is that both prediction set error and prediction set size are small. So can the author draw a clear conclusion for the comparison between Meta-CP and Meta-PS?
2. The authors select PS-Binom to estimate \tau. Do different estimators make difference in the performance? 
3. The score functions in lines 108 and 106 have different input spaces. Can the author explain why? My understanding is that f is a meta-learned off-the-shelf model.
reviews.limitations:
1. The paper is not self-contain, the readers who are not familiar with [1] will have a hard time understanding this paper. I will highly suggest the authors to spent some paragraphs explaining some background. 
2. Only one meta-model, prototypical network, is applied as a score function to evaluate the proposed method. 
3. The notations are very confusing, especially those applied for the score function, and prediction set. 
[1] Park, Sangdon, et al. "PAC confidence sets for deep neural networks via calibrated prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.00106 (2019).
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 2: You are willing to defend your assessment, but it is quite likely that you did not understand the central parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The paper proposed a method for estimating the prediction sets in the meta-test stage from the estimated prediction sets gathered from the meta-train adaptation and calibration phases. The whole process is carefully designed to satisfy the PAC setting in provide a theoretical guarantee for generating the meta-test prediction sets without calibration. The proposed method is tested on four different datasets across three application domains with competitive results.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The proposed method is guaranteed by PAC bound. 

Weaknesses:
1. The paper is not presented in a good way. The notation is very confusing and misleading in general.
2. A big picture is missing. For example, we know that f is a pre-trained meta-model. But in Figure 1 this information is missing which may confuse the meta-learning people.

Questions:
1. In terms of evaluation, I think both predictions set error and prediction set size are considered as the measurements. Among the experiment settings, Meta-PS shows relatively low set error but in some cases set size is larger than others. I think the idea case is that both prediction set error and prediction set size are small. So can the author draw a clear conclusion for the comparison between Meta-CP and Meta-PS?
2. The authors select PS-Binom to estimate \tau. Do different estimators make difference in the performance? 
3. The score functions in lines 108 and 106 have different input spaces. Can the author explain why? My understanding is that f is a meta-learned off-the-shelf model.

Limitations:
1. The paper is not self-contain, the readers who are not familiar with [1] will have a hard time understanding this paper. I will highly suggest the authors to spent some paragraphs explaining some background. 
2. Only one meta-model, prototypical network, is applied as a score function to evaluate the proposed method. 
3. The notations are very confusing, especially those applied for the score function, and prediction set. 
[1] Park, Sangdon, et al. "PAC confidence sets for deep neural networks via calibrated prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.00106 (2019).

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
## Hybrid Review

**Summary:**  
This paper proposes a novel method for constructing PAC prediction sets in meta-learning, enabling uncertainty quantification when adapting to new tasks with minimal labeled examples. The approach leverages meta-train adaptation and calibration phases to estimate prediction sets for the meta-test stage, providing a theoretical guarantee (meta-PAC) that ensures correctness with high probability over future tasks—a significant advancement over prior average guarantees. The method is rigorously evaluated across four diverse datasets: mini-ImageNet and CIFAR10-C (vision), FewRel (NLP), and CDC Heart (healthcare), demonstrating competitive results with smaller prediction sets than baselines like Meta-CP while satisfying error constraints.

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**  
*Strengths:*  
1.  **Theoretical Rigor:** The core strength is the novel meta-PAC guarantee (Theorem 1), providing a rigorous task-conditional bound—a clear differentiation from prior conformal prediction methods.  
2.  **Practical Significance:** The approach addresses a critical need for reliable uncertainty quantification in safety-critical meta-learning applications (e.g., healthcare, autonomous systems).  
3.  **Empirical Validation:** Experiments are thorough, spanning multiple domains and demonstrating the method's ability to satisfy the theoretical guarantee while often achieving smaller prediction sets than strong baselines.  

*Weaknesses:*  
1.  **Clarity and Presentation:** The paper is not presented optimally. The notation is frequently confusing and misleading, particularly regarding the score function and prediction set definitions. Figure 1 lacks crucial details (e.g., depicting `f` as the pre-trained meta-model), hindering comprehension.  
2.  **Accessibility:** The paper is not self-contained. Readers unfamiliar with the foundational PAC work [Park et al., 2020] will struggle, necessitating more background explanation.  
3.  **Limited Scope:** Evaluation relies solely on the Prototypical Network as the meta-learned score function. Exploring other meta-learning architectures would strengthen generalizability claims.  
4.  **Practical Limitations:** While the theoretical guarantee is robust, the method's practical utility depends on sufficient calibration data (`N` tasks, `n` examples). The computational overhead of the calibration process and sensitivity to violations of task exchangeability assumptions require more explicit discussion.  

**Questions:**  
1.  **Meta-PS vs. Meta-CP:** Meta-PS often shows lower set error but sometimes larger set size than Meta-CP. Can the authors provide a clearer comparative analysis? The ideal is small error *and* small size – under what conditions does Meta-PS achieve this balance?  
2.  **Algorithmic Choices:** a) Why was the specific choice of `α/2` for both calibration steps in Algorithm 1 made? Provide theoretical or empirical justification for this split. b) The authors use PS-Binom to estimate `τ`. How sensitive are the results to the choice of estimator?  
3.  **Score Function Design:** The score functions defined in lines 106 and 108 operate on different input spaces. Can the authors clarify the rationale for this design, especially considering `f` is a meta-learned model? Explain the use of a dummy label `1` in constructing `S'`.  
4.  **Empirical Sensitivity:** a) How does performance degrade with fewer calibration tasks (`N`) or samples per task (`n`)? b) What computational overhead does Meta-PS introduce compared to baselines like Meta-CP? c) For the CDC Heart dataset with class imbalance, how were adaptation and evaluation handled for underrepresented or unseen classes?  
5.  **Robustness:** How sensitive is the method to mild violations of the i.i.d. task assumption or distribution shifts? Could the framework incorporate unlabeled test-task data?  

**Limitations:**  
1.  **Clarity and Background:** As noted, the presentation and notation hinder understanding, and insufficient background on PAC prediction sets is provided.  
2.  **Evaluation Breadth:** The reliance on only one meta-learning algorithm (Prototypical Networks) limits the assessment of the method's broader applicability. Comparison to alternative uncertainty quantification approaches like Bayesian meta-learning is absent.  
3.  **Data and Computation:** The theoretical guarantee requires sufficient calibration data (`N`, `n`), which may be impractical in very low-resource scenarios. The computational cost of the calibration process, especially for large `N`, needs explicit acknowledgment.  
4.  **Assumptions and Risks:** The method assumes task exchangeability. Sensitivity analysis regarding violations of this assumption is lacking. While societal impacts (e.g., potential misuse in healthcare due to overreliance on guarantees) are mentioned in the appendix, a more substantive discussion of ethical considerations and failure modes (e.g., performance under significant distribution shift or biased calibration data) within the main text is warranted.  

**Recommendation:**  
**Rating: 5 (Borderline Accept)**  
This paper presents a **technically solid contribution** with a novel and **significant theoretical advance**—the meta-PAC guarantee for prediction sets in meta-learning. The rigorous proof and **empirical validation across diverse domains** are strengths that support acceptance. However, the **presentation deficiencies** (confusing notation, lack of clarity in figures and background, readability issues) and **limited evaluation scope** (single meta-learner, insufficient exploration of practical constraints like computational cost and data efficiency) are substantial weaknesses. While the core idea and results are promising and advance the field, the current presentation hinders accessibility and impact. **Reasons to accept (novelty, theoretical contribution, multi-domain validation) outweigh reasons to reject (presentation, scope), but only marginally.** Addressing the clarity issues and expanding the discussion of limitations in a revision would significantly strengthen the paper.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The paper proposed a method for estimating the prediction sets in the meta-test stage from the estimated prediction sets gathered from the meta-train adaptation and calibration phases. The whole process is carefully designed to satisfy the PAC setting in provide a theoretical guarantee for generating the meta-test prediction sets without calibration. The proposed method is tested on four different datasets across three application domains with competitive results.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The proposed method is guaranteed by PAC bound. 

Weaknesses:
1. The paper is not presented in a good way. The notation is very confusing and misleading in general.
2. A big picture is missing. For example, we know that f is a pre-trained meta-model. But in Figure 1 this information is missing which may confuse the meta-learning people.

Questions:
1. In terms of evaluation, I think both predictions set error and prediction set size are considered as the measurements. Among the experiment settings, Meta-PS shows relatively low set error but in some cases set size is larger than others. I think the idea case is that both prediction set error and prediction set size are small. So can the author draw a clear conclusion for the comparison between Meta-CP and Meta-PS?
2. The authors select PS-Binom to estimate \tau. Do different estimators make difference in the performance? 
3. The score functions in lines 108 and 106 have different input spaces. Can the author explain why? My understanding is that f is a meta-learned off-the-shelf model.

Limitations:
1. The paper is not self-contain, the readers who are not familiar with [1] will have a hard time understanding this paper. I will highly suggest the authors to spent some paragraphs explaining some background. 
2. Only one meta-model, prototypical network, is applied as a score function to evaluate the proposed method. 
3. The notations are very confusing, especially those applied for the score function, and prediction set. 
[1] Park, Sangdon, et al. "PAC confidence sets for deep neural networks via calibrated prediction." arXiv preprint arXiv:2001.00106 (2019).

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

title: Grounded Reinforcement Learning: Learning to Win the Game under Human Commands

reviews.summary:
Paper introduces a new task called “Grounded Reinforcement Learning” in which agents must be reward-maximizing with respect to some high-level language “constraint” (given by another agent/human). The authors demonstrate that existing vanilla RL, constrained RL (CRL), and behavior cloning (BC) fail to perform well both with and without commands in the MiniRTS environment (an example of this problem setup).

To solve this problem setting, the authors propose a new method RED - Reinforced Demonstration Distillation, which repeatedly alternates between pure RL and BC updates.  The authors demonstrate that RED is both the strongest and most grounded policy. The authors provide, through ablations, compelling arguments.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:

Introduces (somewhat) of a new problem statement

Clearly written 

Human Evaluation is compelling

Impactful work from a safety perspective. 

Weaknesses:

My largest criticism is the use of the word "language" and i think this undermines the entire approach - the current representation treats commands as one-hot representations. These are not learnt, shaped or any different to the state space. This has no standard components which i would consider natural language, such as pragmatics, syntax and semantics.

RED algorithm does not seem novel (effectively mixture of two)

Results are not significantly better than baselines.
reviews.questions:
1) It is unclear how this approach is different from having standard RL with a combined state space over both command and game state.  As the command representation is not learnt - it seems unclear how this actually is different to a standard RL problem.

2) Can more justification be given to how difficult the opponent you play with is? Can you play with a harder agent to more clearly demonstrate the advantages of your method?

3) It is really unclear how the command token is meaningfully different to having an additional dimension within the game state. Can some justification be given to how either this representation is learnt or has any assemblance of language (is it compositional, have semantic or pragmatic properties).
reviews.limitations:
Addressing the above questions would be useful. 

I also believe actually using a language model would take great steps in demonstrating the effectiveness of your approach.
reviews.rating: 7: Accept: Technically solid paper, with high impact on at least one sub-area, or moderate-to-high impact on more than one areas, with good-to-excellent evaluation, resources, reproducibility, and no unaddressed ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review: Grounded Reinforcement Learning via Reinforced Demonstration Distillation  

### Summary  
This paper introduces **Grounded Reinforcement Learning (GRL)**, a novel problem where an agent must maximize task performance (e.g., winning a real-time strategy game) while strictly adhering to high-level natural language commands, even when sub-optimal. The authors formalize this via a constrained RL objective and propose **RED (REinforced demonstration Distillation)**, an iterative algorithm alternating between unconstrained RL (for strategy discovery) and behavior cloning (BC) over human demonstrations (for command adherence). Evaluations in the MiniRTS environment demonstrate RED’s superiority over baselines (vanilla RL, constrained RL, behavior cloning, joint training) in both win rate and command obedience, validated through rigorous simulations and human studies. Ablations provide compelling arguments for RED’s design.  

### Strengths  
1. **Original Problem Formulation**: GRL addresses a critical underexplored challenge—balancing strategic competence with strict command adherence—and clearly differentiates from prior language grounding or imitation learning. This is impactful for human-AI collaboration, particularly from a safety perspective.  
2. **Technical Soundness**: The constrained RL objective is well-motivated, and experiments are thorough, including multiple baselines, ablation studies, and human evaluations. Metrics (win rates, success rates, obedience) robustly support claims.  
3. **Clarity**: The paper is well-structured and clearly written, with figures effectively illustrating concepts.  
4. **Practical Significance**: RED advances SOTA by handling sub-optimal commands/demonstrations where baselines fail, enabling performant yet obedient agents.  

### Weaknesses  
1. **Language Representation**: The treatment of commands as **one-hot vectors** (not learned or compositional) lacks standard linguistic properties (pragmatics, syntax, semantics). This undermines the "language grounding" claim and resembles an augmented state space.  
2. **Algorithmic Novelty**: RED’s design—alternating RL and BC—lacks theoretical justification and resembles existing hybrid approaches (e.g., SSIL [42] is notably absent from comparisons).  
3. **Limited Evaluation Scope**: Results are confined to MiniRTS; broader validation (e.g., StarCraft II) is needed to prove scalability to complex state/action spaces or richer language.  
4. **Theoretical Gaps**: The intuitive justification for RED’s convergence (Sec 4.3) lacks formal guarantees, especially regarding distribution shift during distillation.  

### Questions  
1. **Command vs. State Space**: How is the command token fundamentally different from an additional state dimension? Justify why this representation constitutes "language" (e.g., compositionality, semantics) rather than a discrete signal.  
2. **Scalability**: How does RED scale to environments with larger action spaces, longer horizons, or more complex language? What compute/data would be needed?  
3. **Baseline Competitiveness**: Why does RED outperform Joint training despite similar obedience (Table 1)? Is the win-rate gap under adversarial commands confounded by inherent policy strength?  
4. **Constraint Sensitivity**: Why was BC loss chosen as the constraint metric over learned alternatives? How was the δ threshold determined, and can it adapt to extremely sub-optimal commands?  
5. **Opponent Difficulty**: Can justification be provided for the opponent’s skill level? Would a harder adversary amplify RED’s advantages?  

### Limitations  
1. **Language Abstraction**: The one-hot command representation limits real-world applicability. Incorporating language models (e.g., LLMs) could enhance generalization to OOD commands and true linguistic grounding.  
2. **Scalability and Sim-to-Real Gaps**: MiniRTS’s simplicity may not reflect real-world complexity. Broader domain validation is essential.  
3. **Societal Risks**: While briefly noted, risks of **blind obedience** (e.g., in safety-critical systems) need deeper ethical analysis. Mitigation strategies for harmful/ambiguous commands are unexplored.  
4. **Theoretical Underpinnings**: RED’s efficacy lacks formal guarantees for convergence or constraint satisfaction under distribution shift.  

### Recommendation  
**Rating: 7 (Accept)**  
This is a **technically solid paper** with **moderate-to-high impact** on human-AI collaboration and safe RL. The novel GRL formulation and RED’s empirical success are strengths, supported by good evaluation and reproducibility. However, weaknesses in language representation, scalability, and theoretical rigor prevent a higher rating. Addressing the raised questions—especially regarding linguistic grounding and scalability—would strengthen future work.  

---  
**Hybrid Integration Notes**:  
- **Language Critique** (Main Review) merged with **Scalability/Theory** (Alternatives 1–4).  
- **Strengths** expanded with "originality" and "significance" (Alternatives 1,2,4) but tempered by language weakness.  
- **Questions** combine Main’s focus on command representation with Alternatives’ scalability/constraint sensitivity.  
- **Limitations** incorporate societal risks (Alternatives 1,3,4) and LLM suggestion (Alternative 1) while preserving Main’s core concern.  
- **Rating** unchanged; weaknesses justify 7 despite Alternatives’ higher suggestions.  
- **Tone**: Professional and cohesive, with seamless transitions between integrated critiques.

reviews.summary:
This work focuses on grounded reinforcement learning. Generally, it means finding a conditional RL policy that can perfectly response to high-level command. 

In this paper, the authors proposed a constraned RL algorithm RED to (1) train an agent that is capable of winning strategy games and (2) follow most human instructions. Concretely, RED uses RL to learn explorative policy while uses BC to imitate human demonstration.

Experiment results suggest RED achieves command-following policies and higher winning rate.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
### Strengths

* The idea is concise. Use BC to learn human behavior while use RL to explore better solution.
* Visualization and videos provided are mostly clear and contain necessary information.
* Fig1 is illustrative and helpful to explain what is grounded reinforcement learning. (It would be better if Fig 1 has no background color and in PDF format)
* Code is released.

### Weaknesses

* This work assumes reward from interactive environment. (limitation)
* The code has no detailed guidance and documentation.
* Though I like Figure 2 and other experiments, the main experiment results in Table 1 is confusing and non-convincing.
* The idea is not novel. Conditional imitation learning is a long lasting topics. Refer to "End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning"
* Many details are missing or confusing, such as Table 1 and Lagrangian method.
reviews.questions:
### Questions on method


Sec 4.3 says this work uses Lagrangian multiplier to solve the constrained optimization problem in Eq2. It turns out that, in my understanding, this is simply a weighted loss. A formal Lagrangian method should contain update process of the $\beta$, which is just ignored by this paper (or I miss, please point out). The later "iterative solution" also breaks the so called "Lagrangian method". 

---

Line 240 and Appendix A.2: Could you discuss more details on how you deal with action space? What does the "0/1 action for each unit" means? Who does the selection?

---

How to insert a NA command? IIUC, there exist a fixed set of possible commands right? So NA command should be just one more command in that set. You create embedding for each command in the set and than pass the embedding to the neural network.

---





### Questions on experiments

I can't understand table 1. IIUC, these four baselines have the policies that do not take command as input. Why they have different performance in Table 1 when applying different test commands? Please describe how you turn them as conditional policies. 

For example, since RL is trained without any objective to conduct distinguishable behaviors under different commands, why inserting different test commands will lead to different behavior?

---

I don't think Table 1 is convincing, especially when taking STD into account. 

Line 288: "Both RED and Joint policies substantially outperform Switch (BC) policy with sub-optimal commands (Random and Human Proxy), suggesting the conditioned behaviors are improved." This is a overclaimed statement. I can't draw this conclusion from Table 1.

---

I like Fig 2, which shows the dependency between commands and behaviors. Many "conditional RL" methods fail to prove that the conditional policy has casuality to the input command. This figure also shows that RED has much better "casuality" than RL.

My problem is that, in Figure 2, RL in 0% random commands achieves ~57% win rate. Does this value align with the RL performance with NA test command in Table 1?

Again, if RL is trained compelety without human command, then (1) where you can insert the command to the policy? and (2) why RL performance changes with the percentage of random commands?

---


Figure 6 says RL policy achieves similar win rate compared to RED. IIUC, RL policies are not conditioned on any human command. This result suggest that a human subject cooperates with RED trained conditional policy will lead to RL-comparable performance? This seems to be a violation to Table 1 "Oracle" result, which might lead to 

---

There is only one real gameplay footage provided (less than 2 minutes), with only 6 commands given, which is far less than convincing. Meanwhile, the commands seemed to be programmed/copy-pasted (I did not see any input lag), is there any real human intervention involved in recoding this video?

---

This game seems to be very complicated. Does your first intention of the application of this algorithm to be this high-level game? I understand the miniRTS environment is the only one that fitted the algorithm's need, but if you have alternatives (even if the performance is weak), would you also like to present them in the paper? Having diversity is a huge plus to your paper.








### Questions on paper




### Other questions or concerns



The [google drive link](https://drive.google.com/file/d/129ZV9CU6zUnp5B4v0p91Xaae8-M5zxHy/view) provided is **not anonymous**. This is a violation of the NeurIPS Code of Conduct (double-blind reviewing policy). Please action ASAP.

---

This paper has experiments involving 30 college students experiments, are attendees aware of their rights and responsibilities? A more explicit Ethics Statement should be included.

---

Conditional BC is not a new idea. Please refer to this work: End-to-end Driving via Conditional Imitation Learning

---

Appendix D, as said in the checklist, has no information on human experiment. You moved them to Appendix E.

---

The website given in the appendix is very informative and I like the real-time gameplay footage, but there are several problems I want to point out:
- Code link is broken.
- Many grammar errors, will not be present here (plural or singular, tense, etc)
reviews.limitations:
The authors provide limitation section.

GRL is an interesting field. How to improve human-AI cooperation in test-time, how to learn a better human proxy, and how to further encourage, illustrate, quantify the causity between command and behaviors might be future directions.
