reviews.summary:
In this paper the authors propose the “Concept Recursive Activation FacTorization” (CRAFT), a framework to generate concept-based explanations from trained networks. CRAFT aims at addressing current limitations in state-of-the-art concept-based techniques (e.g. ACE [1]) including: (i) the association of *one concept only* for each image segment/patch, (ii) the instability/noise of gradients w.r.t. concept vectors, (iii) the localization of concepts in the input space to provide local explanations. To address these limitations, the authors equip CRAFT with three methods: (i) a recursive procedure to automatically decompose concepts into sub-concepts, (ii) an alternative scoring function for concept importance based on Sobol indeces, and (iii) a method to localize concepts in the input (pixel) space. Following Zhang et al. [2], CRAFT identifies coherent bases of concepts using the Non-negative Matrix Factorization (NMF) rather than k-Means as in ACE [1]. In the experiments the authors show that CRAFT can: (i) identify meaningful and coherent concepts, (ii) localize concepts in the input space allowing users to explain misclassified images, (iii) decompose high-level concepts into coherent subconcepts, and (iv) provide a more robust ranking concept w.r.t. TCAV in terms of  fidelity scores.

[1] Ghorbani, Amirata, et al. "Towards automatic concept-based explanations." Advances in Neural Information Processing Systems 32 (2019).

[2] Zhang, Ruihan, et al. "Invertible concept-based explanations for cnn models with non-negative concept activation vectors." Proceedings of the AAAI Conference on Artificial Intelligence. Vol. 35. No. 13. 2021.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
#  Strengths
The automatic decomposition of high-level concepts into sub-concepts is the most interesting contribution of this paper w.r.t. existing concept-based approaches. This is a significant step forward w.r.t. state-of-the-art approaches such as ACE as it enables the identification of coherent hierarchies of concepts. In particular concept hierarchies may facilitate human experts in navigating the concept space. Even though the methods are not new (e.g. NMF, or Sobol indeces), they are effectively used in new contexts. In addition, the experiments show how CRAFT can be used in practice as a powerful tool to “debug” misclassified samples by localize concepts in the input space.

# Limitations
While this paper introduces a few interesting steps forward, the authors may consider a few suggestions for improving their contributions. In particular the authors may consider to:
1. Broaden the experimental section as it is currently based on one dataset only, thus questioning the generality of the results. I would strongly encourage the authors to broaden their experiments to include additional benchmarks commonly used in concept-based XAI papers (e.g. CUB [1], TabulaMuris [2], or CelebA [3]).
2. Include a description for the limitations of their work. Including relevant limitations would significantly improve the paper as it would allow other scientists to understand how to use and improve CRAFT advancing the research field. A possible limitation worth mentioning is that the experimental evidence is mostly qualitative (Section 4.1, 4.2, and 4.3) and/or possibly subject to confirmation or selection biases (Section 4.3).
3. Include critical information which is currently missing for understanding and reproducing the experiments. For instance, the authors do not mention what error bars represent (e.g. Figure 6) nor how the images where selected for the human experiments i.e., they just mention that “the choice was randomized” (line 701), but they do not specify how (e.g. random selection from cherry-picked examples or from all images? From groups of image crops or from the original images?). Another issue is that it is not clear how the authors pick CRAFT hyperparameters (e.g. $\tau$, line 263) and how sensitive the results are to hyperparameters’ changes.
4. Reorganize some sections, include missing information, or rephrase a few sentences to improve flow and clarity. For instance, in the abstract the authors do not mention any knowledge gap which makes a bit unclear why CRAFT might be needed. On the contrary, in the introduction the authors mention four knowledge gaps (lines 31, 41, 47, and 51) which makes a bit unclear what is the *main* purpose of CRAFT. Making the introduction and contributions more focused would significantly improve flow and clarity and it would save the authors some space for describing methods, results, and limitations.

[1] Wah, Catherine, et al. "The caltech-ucsd birds-200-2011 dataset." (2011).

[2] Cao, Kaidi, Maria Brbic, and Jure Leskovec. "Concept learners for few-shot learning." arXiv preprint arXiv:2007.07375 (2020).

[3] Liu, Ziwei, et al. "Large-scale celebfaces attributes (celeba) dataset." Retrieved August 15.2018 (2018): 11.
reviews.questions:
Addressing the main limitations mentioned above would significantly improve the paper and, as a consequence, reviewers’ feedback and scores. However, I understand that the authors may not have enough time to address them all in the rebuttal. For this reason I list here a few minor items which can give the authors a better chance to improve their work in the short term:
1. A few sentences may need further clarification as they may contain typos or have grammar issues (e.g. ““The second concept elucidated by craft is located on the astronaut’s pants that **he confuse** with the ski suits” (Figure 5)). The authors may also improve the flow using the active voice more frequently (e.g. “Namely, in [38] and [39], explanations are generated as the inputs” (line 97)). Other sentences which may benefit from rephrasing include: “Once the factorization done, and the reconstruction of the activation of the image can then be interpreted as a combination of a set of concepts and a coefficient associated to these concepts.” (line 116), “A common concern with concept extraction methods is that make sense to humans does not mean it” (line 189), “Finally, we can capture the importance that a concept might have as a main effect – by itself – as well 210 as by interacting with other concepts on the model’s output by calculating the expected variance that 211 would remain if all the indices of the masks except the Mi were to be fixed.” (line 209). 
2. A few acronyms need to be spelled out (e.g. TCAV, line 112).
3. The authors reference Figure 3 before Figure 2. I would suggest to swap the figures.
4. Some claims may require to be rephrased as in their current form may not align with actual results. For instance, I find the possibility of using CRAFT for explaining complex failure cases quite exciting, but the only experimental result is on one cherry-picked example which may not be enough to prove the efficacy of the method (Section 4.2).  Another example is when authors state “We use our approach and combine local and global explanation, to *accurately* explain predictions” (line 72). Here I was expecting a *quantitative* result in the experimental section showing the actual *accuracy* scores of CRAFT’s explanations. A third example is when authors state that “CRAFT yields more interpretable concepts than ACE” (line 74). The main issue here is that ACE and CRAFT are different under many aspects. These differences make a fair comparison a bit tricky as each difference may introduce a confounding factor in the comparison. However, the results here are only qualitative (Figure 4 and appendix) which makes the claim a bit weaker than it could have been. Moreover, the experiment design does not allow to identify which ingredient (i.e. Sobol indeces? NMF? or $\tau$?) from ACE or CRAFT is the one that is actually making the difference. A fourth example is when authors state “These examples illustrate one of the weaknesses of ACE: the segmentation used can introduce biases through the baseline value used. Moreover, this segmentation does not facilitate the interpretation of the concepts” (line 276). However the evidence here is only based on a qualitative visual comparison which may not be enough to support such strong claims.

reviews.text:
**Hybrid Review**

**Summary**  
This paper introduces CRAFT, a framework for generating concept-based explanations in deep neural networks. The authors propose three key components: (1) a recursive decomposition method to hierarchically break down high-level concepts into sub-concepts, addressing neural collapse in late layers; (2) Sobol indices for robust concept importance estimation; and (3) implicit differentiation for generating concept attribution maps. Experiments on ImageNet-classified ResNet50 models demonstrate improved fidelity over TCAV/ACE baselines, human-validated concept coherence via psychophysics studies, and case studies for failure mode analysis. While the technical integration of NMF with recursive decomposition and sensitivity analysis is novel, the experimental validation remains limited to a single dataset, and critical methodological details are omitted, raising reproducibility concerns.

**Strengths and Weaknesses**  
*Strengths*:  
The recursive decomposition of concepts is a significant advancement over existing methods like ACE, enabling hierarchical concept navigation. The integration of Sobol indices provides a theoretically grounded approach to concept importance estimation, addressing gradient instability in prior work. Human psychophysics experiments (n=73) lend empirical support to concept interpretability, and the implicit differentiation method for localization bridges global and local explanations. The ablation studies and fidelity metrics (Fig. 6) demonstrate technical soundness, albeit limited to ResNet/ImageNet.

*Weaknesses*:  
The experimental scope is critically narrow: validation relies solely on ImageNet-derived subsets, omitting standard benchmarks like CUB or CelebA. While human studies are a strength, participant diversity and sample size limit generalizability. Quantitative results are overshadowed by qualitative cherry-picking (e.g., single failure case in Section 4.2). Key methodological gaps persist: hyperparameter sensitivity (e.g., τ, λ), computational costs of recursion, and layer selection heuristics are unanalyzed. Comparisons to recent methods (e.g., Ge et al. 2021) are absent, weakening claims of superiority. Societal impacts, including dataset biases and potential misuse, are unaddressed.

**Limitations**  
The authors must explicitly acknowledge:  
1. **Narrow evaluation**: Results may not generalize beyond ResNet/ImageNet; broader validation across architectures/datasets is needed.  
2. **Qualitative bias**: Case studies (Sections 4.1–4.3) risk confirmation bias without quantitative baselines.  
3. **Reproducibility gaps**: Missing details on error bars (Fig. 6), image selection protocols, and ADMM solver parameters.  
4. **Computational costs**: Recursive decomposition scalability to larger models is unquantified.  
5. **Ethical considerations**: No discussion of privacy risks from ImageNet biases or misuse potential.  

**Questions**  
1. How were hyperparameters (τ, λ) selected, and how sensitive are results to their variation?  
2. What criteria determine the stopping point for recursive decomposition? Could layer-wise Sobol indices automate this?  
3. How does NMF initialization impact concept stability across runs?  
4. Were concepts validated against ground-truth attributes (e.g., CUB’s part annotations)?  
5. Why omit comparisons to recent methods like Ge et al. (CVPR2021)?  
6. What safeguards prevent spurious concepts from propagating through recursion?  

**Recommendation**  
While CRAFT introduces promising technical advances—particularly in recursive decomposition and Sobol-based importance scoring—the limited evaluation and methodological omissions significantly undermine its contributions. The reliance on a single dataset, unaddressed reproducibility gaps, and lack of quantitative baselines for key claims (e.g., failure case explanations) outweigh the framework’s theoretical strengths. In its current form, the paper does not meet the threshold for acceptance. Major revisions addressing these issues could make this work impactful, but as submitted, I recommend **borderline rejection (Rating 4)**.

reviews.summary: The authors have done a nice job by improving the concept based explanation generation techniques from different perspectives. An automatic concept extraction followed by a recursive procedure towards achieving a set of concepts and sub-concepts are proposed in this work. A concept-based heatmap generation method is also proposed here that backpropagate concept values to pixel space that is very similar to widely used class-based saliency methods.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths: 
a) Several important directions are worked on here, that start with concept generation in a recursive manner from multiple layers of the network. This technique helps the cases where concepts generated from a very deep layer don't make much sense and decomposing them with sub-concepts can develop an improved understanding of the model. 

b) This is the first method, I believe, to work on saliency based explanations for each concept. The authors have shown the effectiveness of this approach by explaining different concepts in a single image. 


Weaknesses: 
a) Although the quality of a technical literature shouldn't be judged by the language quality, I still believe that there are many scopes to polish the language and take care of some typos to improve the readability of the paper.

b) I couldn't access the code related to the paper from the provided link. If by any chance the code was not ready for sharing by the submission time, it would have been better to skip that.
reviews.questions:
L31: What do you mean by information methods?

L73-74: If some figure in the supplementary section is referred to in the main paper, it is a good practice to clearly mention that.

L137: I request the authors to explain the notation h_{l,k}, as l and k represent some layer and the total number of layers respectively, but it was not clear to me how they're used in a single notation.

L163: Please write the full form when the short form is used for the first time in the paper. The full form of NNLS (i.e. Non Negative Least Square) should have been mentioned here.

Other questions: 
a) Please explain the notations of Table 1 in detail. It is not very clear what 'n' represents here.

b) Here the concepts found by NMF are claimed to be human understandable. If any or some of the concepts are exceptions, I am interested to know whether these concepts are specific to a particular category?

c) As the gradient based explanation generation techniques generally produce noisy saliency maps, did you see any such phenomenon for concepts? Do the heatmaps for all the images are as nice as shown in fig.1? If yes, which part of the method (related to concept attribution map generation) helped you to achieve that specifically?
reviews.limitations: The authors did not add any section to address the limitations and potential negative societal impact of this work.
reviews.rating: 5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.confidence: 3: You are fairly confident in your assessment. It is possible that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work. Math/other details were not carefully checked.
reviews.full_review_text:
Summary:
The authors have done a nice job by improving the concept based explanation generation techniques from different perspectives. An automatic concept extraction followed by a recursive procedure towards achieving a set of concepts and sub-concepts are proposed in this work. A concept-based heatmap generation method is also proposed here that backpropagate concept values to pixel space that is very similar to widely used class-based saliency methods.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: 
a) Several important directions are worked on here, that start with concept generation in a recursive manner from multiple layers of the network. This technique helps the cases where concepts generated from a very deep layer don't make much sense and decomposing them with sub-concepts can develop an improved understanding of the model. 

b) This is the first method, I believe, to work on saliency based explanations for each concept. The authors have shown the effectiveness of this approach by explaining different concepts in a single image. 


Weaknesses: 
a) Although the quality of a technical literature shouldn't be judged by the language quality, I still believe that there are many scopes to polish the language and take care of some typos to improve the readability of the paper.

b) I couldn't access the code related to the paper from the provided link. If by any chance the code was not ready for sharing by the submission time, it would have been better to skip that.

Questions:
L31: What do you mean by information methods?

L73-74: If some figure in the supplementary section is referred to in the main paper, it is a good practice to clearly mention that.

L137: I request the authors to explain the notation h_{l,k}, as l and k represent some layer and the total number of layers respectively, but it was not clear to me how they're used in a single notation.

L163: Please write the full form when the short form is used for the first time in the paper. The full form of NNLS (i.e. Non Negative Least Square) should have been mentioned here.

Other questions: 
a) Please explain the notations of Table 1 in detail. It is not very clear what 'n' represents here.

b) Here the concepts found by NMF are claimed to be human understandable. If any or some of the concepts are exceptions, I am interested to know whether these concepts are specific to a particular category?

c) As the gradient based explanation generation techniques generally produce noisy saliency maps, did you see any such phenomenon for concepts? Do the heatmaps for all the images are as nice as shown in fig.1? If yes, which part of the method (related to concept attribution map generation) helped you to achieve that specifically?

Limitations:
The authors did not add any section to address the limitations and potential negative societal impact of this work.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
reviews.text:
**Hybrid Review**  

**Summary**  
The authors present CRAFT, a framework advancing concept-based explanation generation through recursive decomposition of concepts across network layers, Sobol indices for concept importance estimation, and implicit differentiation for concept attribution maps. Building on NMF foundations, the method addresses neural collapse in deep layers by recursively decomposing high-level concepts into interpretable sub-concepts. The proposed concept-based heatmap generation, which backpropagates concept values to pixel space, demonstrates promising results comparable to class-based saliency methods. While the technical contributions are novel and human psychophysics experiments validate concept coherence, broader architectural validation and computational cost analysis remain underexplored.

**Strengths and Weaknesses**  
*Strengths*  
a) The recursive decomposition mechanism innovatively bridges global and local explanations by addressing the semantic degradation of concepts in deeper layers. This hierarchical approach advances unsupervised concept extraction beyond prior work like ACE/TCAV.  

b) The integration of Sobol indices for concept importance estimation and implicit differentiation for attribution maps constitutes a methodological leap, enabling both "what" and "where" explanations. Human studies (n=73) quantitatively validate improved interpretability over baselines.  

c) The application to model debugging (e.g., failure mode analysis in Fig. 1) demonstrates practical utility. Fidelity metrics show statistical improvements over TCAV, supported by ablation studies.  

*Weaknesses*  
a) While technically solid, comparisons with recent concept discovery methods (e.g., Ge et al. 2021) are limited. The paper would benefit from evaluating against a wider range of baselines to substantiate state-of-the-art claims.  

b) Critical implementation details are omitted: ADMM solver parameters for NMF, layer selection heuristics, and computational costs of recursive decomposition. The lack of code availability (noted in the main review) further hampers reproducibility.  

c) Language clarity issues persist, particularly in mathematical sections (e.g., Section 3.4 derivation of implicit differentiation). Figures like the recursion workflow (Fig. 2/3) could be more pedagogically structured.  

**Questions**  
1. L31: Clarify "information methods" with concrete examples.  
2. L73-74: Explicitly denote supplementary figure references in the main text.  
3. L137: Define notation \( h_{l,k} \) regarding layer indices \( l \) and \( k \).  
4. L163: Expand NNLS (Non-Negative Least Squares) upon first use.  
5. How was the stopping criterion determined for recursive decomposition? Could Sobol indices automate layer selection?  
6. Table 1: Elaborate on parameter \( n \) and address whether concepts showing poor human interpretability (Fig. 4) are category-specific.  
7. What safeguards prevent spurious concept propagation during recursion? How scalable is implicit differentiation to larger models?  

**Limitations**  
The work omits three critical discussions:  
1) *Computational Costs*: No analysis of recursion's time/memory complexity, crucial for scalability to architectures beyond ResNet.  
2) *Societal Impacts*: Potential misuse for "explainability washing" or ImageNet bias propagation (e.g., personally identifiable information) are unaddressed.  
3) *Generalization*: Validation is limited to ImageNet-derived subsets and vision models. Performance on non-ReLU architectures or non-visual domains remains untested.  

**Rating**  
**5: Borderline Accept**  
While the paper makes novel contributions in concept recursion and attribution mapping, its limited evaluation scope (single architecture/dataset), insufficient baseline comparisons, and omitted implementation details marginally outweigh its technical strengths. Addressing these through expanded experiments and broader methodological contextualization would strengthen a resubmission.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
The authors have done a nice job by improving the concept based explanation generation techniques from different perspectives. An automatic concept extraction followed by a recursive procedure towards achieving a set of concepts and sub-concepts are proposed in this work. A concept-based heatmap generation method is also proposed here that backpropagate concept values to pixel space that is very similar to widely used class-based saliency methods.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
Strengths: 
a) Several important directions are worked on here, that start with concept generation in a recursive manner from multiple layers of the network. This technique helps the cases where concepts generated from a very deep layer don't make much sense and decomposing them with sub-concepts can develop an improved understanding of the model. 

b) This is the first method, I believe, to work on saliency based explanations for each concept. The authors have shown the effectiveness of this approach by explaining different concepts in a single image. 


Weaknesses: 
a) Although the quality of a technical literature shouldn't be judged by the language quality, I still believe that there are many scopes to polish the language and take care of some typos to improve the readability of the paper.

b) I couldn't access the code related to the paper from the provided link. If by any chance the code was not ready for sharing by the submission time, it would have been better to skip that.

Questions:
L31: What do you mean by information methods?

L73-74: If some figure in the supplementary section is referred to in the main paper, it is a good practice to clearly mention that.

title: Reaching Nirvana: Maximizing the Margin in Both Euclidean and Angular Spaces for Deep Neural Network Classification

reviews.summary: The paper studies an interesting problem of fixing a set of equi-distance classifiers and then learning features to the corresponding class center (i.e., a vertice in a simplex). The specific form of the loss function is simple and aims to minimize the Euclidean distance between learned feature and the class center (on a hypersphere). This will automatically guarantee maximum separation of class centers and also lead to discriminative feature representation. The paper uses this method in both closed-set and open-set recognition, and shows some improvements.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
I general, I think this is an interesting idea by taking advantages of the simplex and using it as a set of classifiers to guide the feature learning.

Strengths:

- The core idea is interesting. Connecting the simplex and maximum separated classifiers is interesting. Using it as an inductive bias to guide the feature learning is intuitive and seems to make lot of senses.

- The paper also considers the weakness of this method due to the limitation in dimension. The propose dimension augmentaion module aims to address this limitation (however, it inevitably introduces additional trainable parameters and it might not be a fair comparison to the other baselines any more).

- The paper considers both closed-set and open-set recognition and show some improvements over existing baselines. 

Weaknesses:

- As the authors have mentioned, the biggest weakness of this method is its restriction to feature dimension. Although the dimension augmentation module can partially address this, it does not solve the problem. For example, if you have a million-level classification (e.g., many face recognition datasets have this size), you have to map the feature to a similar scale of dimension, which is computationally intractable and introduces many additional overhead. This limitation largely restricts the application of the proposed method.

- Some important references are missing from the paper. What the simplex is actually doing is to encourage hyperspherical uniformity. This have a large body of works on this, e.g. [1], [2], [3]. I think they are closely related, since the simplex classifier is essentially a special case of hyperspherical uniformity (I understand that the construction procedure could vary).

- The empirical gain seems to be somewhat marginal, especially on closed-set recognition.

[1] Learning towards Minimum Hyperspherical Energy, NeurIPS 2018

[2] Learning with Hyperspherical Uniformity, AISTATS 2021

[3] Regularizing Neural Networks via Minimizing Hyperspherical Energy, CVPR 2020
reviews.questions:
- I am wondering whether the authors only uses the proposed Euclidean loss in closed-set recognition (essentially center loss on a simplex)? It is a bit difficult to believe that it can actually outperform Softmax-based margin losses (say ArcFace). Using only Euclidean losses is actually ineffective in classification problems in my experience (it may work for clustering problems like face verification).

-  In the CIFAR-10 experiment, what is feature dimension in the proposed method? How the simplex is constructed if you are using the same feature dimension as the others? Are the authors using 9-dimension features?
reviews.rating: 6: Weak Accept: Technically solid, moderate-to-high impact paper, with no major concerns with respect to evaluation, resources, reproducibility, ethical considerations.
reviews.confidence: 4: You are confident in your assessment, but not absolutely certain. It is unlikely, but not impossible, that you did not understand some parts of the submission or that you are unfamiliar with some pieces of related work.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review: Interleaved Critique  

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces a novel loss function for deep neural network classifiers that simultaneously maximizes margins in both Euclidean and angular spaces by enforcing class samples to cluster around centers positioned at the vertices of a regular simplex inscribed in a hypersphere. The method requires no hyperparameters for standard closed-set classification and achieves state-of-the-art performance in open-set recognition by creating compact class acceptance regions. While the geometric approach is innovative and leverages high-dimensional properties of simplex structures, its application is constrained by feature dimensionality requirements. The proposed Dimension Augmentation Module (DAM) partially addresses this limitation but introduces scalability trade-offs.  

**Strengths:**  
- **Novel Geometric Foundation:** The core idea of using simplex vertices as class centers is theoretically grounded and provides an elegant solution for joint Euclidean-angular margin maximization. This hyperparameter-free design simplifies implementation for closed-set tasks.  
- **Effective Open-Set Recognition:** The method achieves SOTA results on open-set benchmarks due to compact class regions, significantly improving unknown sample rejection.  
- **Proactive Limitation Mitigation:** The authors acknowledge dimensionality constraints (d ≥ C-1) and propose DAM to extend applicability. Ablation studies (e.g., Fig 3) validate key design choices.  
- **Dual-Space Consistency:** Ensuring equivalent performance under both Euclidean and cosine metrics is a distinctive advantage over existing angular-margin methods.  

**Weaknesses:**  
- **Scalability Limitations:** DAM’s underperformance in large-scale face verification (Table 3) indicates unresolved challenges. For million-class tasks, mapping features to high dimensions becomes computationally intractable and introduces unfair comparisons to lightweight baselines.  
- **Marginal Closed-Set Gains:** Improvements on closed-set tasks are inconsistent, with notable gains only on CIFAR-100. Performance remains below SOTA in large-scale face recognition despite DAM.  
- **Incomplete Theoretical Context:** Key references on hyperspherical uniformity (e.g., NeurIPS 2018 [1], CVPR 2020 [3]) are missing. The simplex classifier should be explicitly positioned within this established literature.  
- **Ethical Oversight:** Societal impacts of enhanced open-set recognition (e.g., surveillance misuse, biased rejection of underrepresented groups) are unaddressed.  
- **Implementation Gaps:** DAM’s architecture lacks critical details (layer dimensions, activations), hindering reproducibility. The fixed hypersphere radius (u=64) also requires justification.  

**Questions for Authors:**  
1. **Loss Function Efficacy:** In closed-set recognition, is the Euclidean loss alone sufficient to outperform Softmax-based margin losses (e.g., ArcFace)? How does performance degrade without DAM for CIFAR-10’s 9-dimensional features?  
2. **DAM Optimization:** Why does DAM underperform in large-scale face verification? What architectural modifications (e.g., alternative layers/activations) were explored? Provide ablation studies.  
3. **Parameter Sensitivity:** Justify the heuristics for open-set loss (λ=1/(2×batch_size²), m=u/2). How sensitive are results to these choices?  
4. **Societal Impact:** What safeguards could prevent misuse of the rejection mechanism? Discuss potential biases in unknown sample detection.  
5. **Theoretical Constraints:** For C >> d, does DAM guarantee simplex feasibility? Validate theoretical bounds and discuss failure cases where simplex geometry hinders representation.  

**Recommendation:**  
**Rating: 6 (Weak Accept)**  
While the paper presents an original approach with strong open-set results and valuable geometric insights, its impact is tempered by scalability limitations and marginal closed-set gains. The DAM’s inefficiency in large-scale tasks and lack of ethical discussion further constrain broader applicability. Nevertheless, the hyperparameter-free design and theoretical foundation warrant acceptance pending:  
- Deeper analysis of DAM’s failure modes  
- Explicit discussion of societal implications  
- Integration with hyperspherical energy literature  

**Improvements for Camera-Ready:**  
1. Add DAM architecture specifics and large-scale optimization studies.  
2. Discuss limitations in societal impact section.  
3. Benchmark against hyperspherical uniformity methods [1–3].  
4. Clarify notation in Eqs 1–2 (undefined vector dimensions).

reviews.summary:
This paper proposed a method to combine the margin maximization in both Euclidean and angular spaces. More specifically, the method maps the labels to the vertices of a regular simplex as new labels.

Section 2: 
1) explains the motivation of the method, i.e. the two distances are the same once the data lie on the boundary of a hyper-sphere. 
2) more details about the method, including the vertices of a regular simplex, the case that uses the background class samples and the Dimension Augmentation Module(which is used for increasing the dimension, since the method requires the dimension of data is nearly the data size).

Section 3 and Appendix provide more details about the experiments:
1) feature representations learned by different loss function.
2) results of Open Set Recognition and Closed Set Recognition.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
*The proposed method is interesting(also the DAM is interesting) and simple, and the simplicity brings several advantages (few hyper-parameters, proper acceptance regions, suit for unbalanced datasets), thus can be easily applied for classification problems. The experiments show that the method achieves good accuracy in several cases (especially the Open Set Recognition).
*The paper is well organized(especially the Section 2, which explains the main idea of the paper).

*Although the paper explains the benefits of the setting, it may need more explains or experiments or theorems to support the method. It's hard to convince me that the method works well now.
*The DAM seems need large size when the C is large.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review  

### Summary  
This paper proposes a novel method for deep neural network classifiers that simultaneously maximizes margins in both Euclidean and angular spaces by enforcing class samples to cluster around centers positioned at the vertices of a regular simplex inscribed in a hypersphere. The approach ensures equivalent pairwise distances between centers, enabling consistent performance with either distance metric during inference. Key contributions include a hyperparameter-free loss for closed-set classification, state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance in open-set recognition (OSR) due to compact class acceptance regions, and a Dimension Augmentation Module (DAM) to address feature-dimension constraints (where *d* ≥ *C*-1 is required). Experiments demonstrate strong accuracy on OSR benchmarks and competitive closed-set results on moderate-scale datasets like CIFAR-100, though limitations emerge in large-scale face recognition when using DAM.  

### Strengths  
1. **Novelty & Simplicity**: The dual-space margin maximization via simplex geometry is innovative and elegantly simple. This simplicity yields practical advantages: no tuned hyperparameters for closed-set tasks, suitability for unbalanced datasets, and easily interpretable acceptance regions for OSR.  
2. **Empirical Performance**: The method achieves SOTA results on multiple OSR benchmarks (Table 1), highlighting its strength in creating separable and compact class regions. Ablation studies (e.g., feature visualizations in Fig. 3) effectively validate design choices.  
3. **Well-Structured Presentation**: The paper is clearly organized, with Section 2 providing a coherent theoretical motivation and methodology. Visualizations aid understanding of the geometric framework.  

### Weaknesses & Limitations  
1. **Insufficient Theoretical/Experimental Justification**: While the geometric framework is promising, the paper lacks rigorous theoretical analysis or ablations to fully substantiate *why* the method works, especially regarding hypersphere radius (*u*) initialization and sensitivity of margin parameters (e.g., *m* in Eq. 5). Claims of independence from data imbalance also require validation.  
2. **DAM Scalability & Reproducibility**: DAM underperforms in large-scale face verification (Table 3), suggesting unresolved scalability issues. Architectural details (e.g., layer dimensions, activations) are insufficient for reproducibility, and no ablations explore DAM’s failure modes or redesign potential.  
3. **Societal Impact Oversight**: Ethical considerations—such as potential misuse of OSR for surveillance or bias in rejection mechanisms—are absent. This warrants discussion given the method’s OSR strengths.  
4. **Narrow Closed-Set Gains**: Closed-set improvements are marginal except on CIFAR-100, and the method does not advance SOTA in large-scale tasks.  

### Questions & Suggestions for Revision  
1. **Hyperparameters & Initialization**: Clarify how *u* (hypersphere radius) and *m* (background margin) were chosen. Provide sensitivity analyses for *u*, *m*, and *λ* (Eq. 5). What justifies "completely random" initialization?  
2. **DAM Effectiveness**:  
   - Why does DAM underperform in large-scale face verification despite resolving dimensionality? Provide error analysis and architectural ablations (e.g., alternative layers/activations).  
   - Compare DAM’s efficiency (training/inference overhead) and performance to methods like [1] (almost-equidistant sets).  
3. **Theoretical Guarantees**: Does DAM ensure simplex feasibility when *C* ≫ *d*? Provide theoretical bounds or failure-case analysis.  
4. **Societal Impact**: Address ethical risks (e.g., biased unknown-class rejection) and propose safeguards.  
5. **Experimental Gaps**:  
   - Validate claims that angular losses fail on non-face tasks using relevant datasets.  
   - Explain unequal within-class distances in Fig. 4 despite theoretical equivalence.  

### Limitations  
The authors acknowledge dimensionality constraints and DAM’s limitations in large-scale tasks (Sec. 3.3.2, 4). However, deeper analysis of DAM’s shortcomings, societal implications, and closed-set scalability is needed.  

### Recommendation  
**Rating: 5 (Borderline Accept)**  
The paper presents a technically solid and innovative approach with compelling OSR results and a hyperparameter-free closed-set loss. However, weaknesses in theoretical grounding, DAM scalability, and reproducibility—coupled with unaddressed societal impacts—outweigh its strengths. The method’s simplicity and OSR advances support acceptance, but **major revisions** addressing the above concerns are essential for impact.  

---  
**Decision**: Borderline Accept  
**Confidence**: Medium  
**Detailed Suggestions**: Prioritize DAM analysis, hyperparameter justifications, and societal impact discussion.

5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.

reviews.summary: This paper introduces a Deep Simplex Classiﬁer (DSC) that maximizes the inter-class margins in both Euclidean and angular spaces for the open set recognition problem. Specifically, this method regards the vertices of a regular C-simplex in the (C-1)-dimensional feature space as the class centers of known C classes in the dataset and then encourages the class samples to be as close to their corresponding centers as possible. In cases that the feature dimension is smaller than C-1, a Dimension Augmentation Module (DAM) is further proposed to expand the feature dimension to C-1 to make it possible to construct the C-simplex. The open set and closed set recognition experiments are conducted on Mnist, Cifar10, SVHN, Cifar100, and Tiny ImageNet datasets while the face recognition experiments are conducted on MS1Mv2 dataset to show the effectiveness of the proposed method.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:
1. The practice of regarding the vertices of a regular C-simplex in (C-1)-dimensional space as the C classes’ centers in the training dataset is new in open set recognition task, which has a clear geometric interpretation. 
2. The proposed method is clearly explained and easy to follow.
3. The authors conduct the illustration experiment to ease the understanding of the effect of DSC and evaluate the proposed method extensively on open set recognition, closed set recognition, and large-scale face recognition tasks.

Weaknesses:
1. The idea of maximizing the inter-class margins by explicitly manipulating the class centers is not new. For fixed centers, in Section 3.2 of [1], Do et al. propose to regard the basis vectors of the C-dimensional space as the C classes’ centers which are also the vertices of a C-simplex. The only difference is that the feature dimension in [1] is one larger than that in this paper. For learnable centers, Hayat et al. [2] also encourage the pairwise distances between any two class centers to be similar in a learnable way to bring uniformly separated centers in Euclidean space. Besides, Liu et al. [3] propose a minimum hyperspherical energy (MHE) regularizer to uniformly distribute the class centers on a hypersphere for maximizing the inter-class separability. These two methods share similar goals with Deep Simplex Classiﬁer (DSC) but do not require that the feature dimension is larger than C-2. As the relations between each pair of classes are different, is it necessary to make the pairwise distances between any two centers identical as in DSC or what is its advantage? I think more discussions and empirical comparisons are needed considering these strongly related existing works. 
2. The motivation of this paper is not clearly expressed yet. The authors claim that the Euclidean and Cosine distances would complement each other in abstract and finally choose to maximize the margin in both the Euclidean and angular spaces in introduction. However, the authors mostly emphasize the complexity of hyperparameters of existing methods in introduction while do not explain how would two kinds of distances complement each other and what is the key advantage of considering both spaces in open set recognition? Besides, the authors simply list and explain a series of existing works one by one in introduction with few summaries, which is kind of redundant, lacks logical coherence, and could be further improved. 
3. The proposed DSC is strictly restricted by the requirement that the feature dimension is not less than C-1. The Dimension Augmentation Module (DAM) would only alleviate this issue when C is small while with a large C, DAM will introduce a large number of parameters. In the face recognition experiment, with 18.6K identities, the number of parameters of the last linear layer in DAM is about 18.6K*18.6K \approx 346M, which is seven times more than the number of parameters of the ResNet-101 backbone (45M) and hinders the practical usage. I think a better solution is needed and essential for the general usage of DSC.
4. The authors claim that DSC does not have any hyperparameters but actually there is a hyperparameter u denoting the radius of the hypersphere. The authors simply set it to 64 following the practice in face recognition, which lacks theoretical and empirical guarantee as this paper does not focus on the task of face recognition and uses datasets beyond face recognition datasets. For this reason, an ablation study is needed to investigate the effect of the value of u. Moreover, the ablation study for the hyperparameters \lambda and m in Equation 5 in open set recognition is also needed. Besides, I also have some concerns about the experiments. First, for the open set recognition task, the proposed DSC uses a large amount of data from 80 Million Tiny Images dataset as the background class in Section 3.2.1, but the existing methods do not adopt this setting, for which I think the comparison in Table 1 is unfair. In line 269, a deeper network is employed for the Tiny ImageNet dataset, is it the same with the settings in existing works? Second, for the closed set recognition task in Table 2, do authors tune the hyperparameters in SphereFace, CosFace, and ArcFace for current classification datasets or directly follow the settings in original papers? Besides, the methods mentioned in the first comment should also be compared in the experiments since they are quite related with the proposed method.

[1] Do, Thanh-Toan, et al. "A theoretically sound upper bound on the triplet loss for improving the efficiency of deep distance metric learning." In CVPR. 2019.
[2] Hayat, Munawar, et al. "Gaussian affinity for max-margin class imbalanced learning." In ICCV. 2019.
[3] Liu, Weiyang, et al. "Learning towards minimum hyperspherical energy." In NeurIPS. 2018.

2. The motivation should be expressed more clearly. 6. For the closed set recognition task in Table 2, do authors tune the hyperparameters in SphereFace, CosFace, and ArcFace for current classification datasets or directly follow the settings in original papers?

The open set and closed set recognition experiments are conducted on Mnist, Cifar10, SVHN, Cifar100, and Tiny ImageNet datasets while the face recognition experiments are conducted on MS1Mv2 dataset to show the effectiveness of the proposed method.

Strengths And Weaknesses:

[3] propose a minimum hyperspherical energy (MHE) regularizer to uniformly distribute the class centers on a hypersphere for maximizing the inter-class separability. These two methods share similar goals with Deep Simplex Classiﬁer (DSC) but do not require that the feature dimension is larger than C-2. As the relations between each pair of classes are different, is it necessary to make the pairwise distances between any two centers identical as in DSC or what is its advantage? I think more discussions and empirical comparisons are needed considering these strongly related existing works. 
2. The motivation of this paper is not clearly expressed yet. The authors claim that the Euclidean and Cosine distances would complement each other in abstract and finally choose to maximize the margin in both the Euclidean and angular spaces in introduction. However, the authors mostly emphasize the complexity of hyperparameters of existing methods in introduction while do not explain how would two kinds of distances complement each other and what is the key advantage of considering both spaces in open set recognition? Besides, the authors simply list and explain a series of existing works one by one in introduction with few summaries, which is kind of redundant, lacks logical coherence, and could be further improved. 
3. The proposed DSC is strictly restricted by the requirement that the feature dimension is not less than C-1. Moreover, the ablation study for the hyperparameters \lambda and m in Equation 5 in open set recognition is also needed. Besides, I also have some concerns about the experiments. reviews.text:
**Hybrid Review**  

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces a Deep Simplex Classifier (DSC) that maximizes inter-class margins in both Euclidean and angular spaces for open set recognition. By positioning class centers at the vertices of a regular C-simplex inscribed on a hypersphere and enforcing compact clustering around these centers, the method achieves consistent performance using either distance metric. A Dimension Augmentation Module (DAM) is proposed to address feature dimension constraints, though it introduces scalability challenges in large-scale tasks. Experiments on standard benchmarks demonstrate competitive closed-set accuracy and state-of-the-art open set recognition results. However, critical limitations in scalability, hyperparameter justification, and experimental fairness significantly undermine the practicality and broader impact of the method.  

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**  
**Strengths:**  
1. The geometric interpretation of class centers as simplex vertices is novel for open set recognition, offering a theoretically grounded approach with clear visualizability (validated by illustrative experiments).  
2. The method achieves strong performance in open set recognition, particularly due to compact class regions that enable reliable unknown sample rejection, advancing the state of the art on benchmarks like CIFAR-100.  
3. The hyperparameter-free design for closed-set classification simplifies implementation compared to existing margin-based methods.  

**Weaknesses:**  
1. **Limited Novelty in Core Idea:** The concept of fixed or uniformly separated class centers has strong precedents in [1–3]. While the simultaneous optimization of Euclidean and angular margins is innovative, the necessity of enforcing identical pairwise distances between centers remains unclear. Prior works like [2] (learnable centers) and [3] (hyperspherical uniformity) achieve similar goals without dimension constraints or excessive parameters, yet these are insufficiently compared or discussed. The claimed dual-space advantage is not rigorously justified beyond superficial references to hyperparameter reduction.  
2. **Scalability and Practicality Concerns:** DAM introduces prohibitive parameter overhead in large-scale tasks (e.g., 346M parameters for 18.6K face identities), degrading performance compared to baseline methods (as shown in face verification experiments). This renders the method impractical for real-world applications with high class counts, a limitation underemphasized by the authors.  
3. **Incomplete Empirical Validation:** Critical hyperparameters (hypersphere radius *u*, loss coefficients *λ/m*) lack ablation studies, with *u=64* adopted from face recognition without justification for other datasets. The open set experiments use 80 Million Tiny Images as background data—an unfair advantage over baselines not employing this strategy. Additionally, the use of deeper networks for Tiny ImageNet without alignment with prior works’ configurations raises concerns about comparison validity.  
4. **Motivation and Presentation Issues:** The introduction redundantly lists existing works without synthesizing their limitations or clearly articulating how dual-space optimization uniquely addresses open set challenges. The claim that Euclidean and angular metrics “complement” each other remains vague, lacking theoretical or empirical grounding.  

**Questions:**  
1. How does the uniform simplex geometry justify its necessity over learnable or hyperspherical center distributions ([2,3]), especially when inter-class relationships are inherently non-uniform? Provide comparative analysis with these methods.  
2. DAM underperforms in large-scale face verification despite resolving dimensionality. What architectural modifications (e.g., alternative activations, layer dimensions) were explored to improve DAM, and can theoretical guarantees for simplex feasibility when *C >> d* be established?  
3. The hypersphere radius *u=64* and loss coefficients *λ/m* are heuristically set. Provide sensitivity analyses to justify these choices across datasets, particularly for non-face recognition tasks.  
4. For open set experiments, why is the 80 Million Tiny Images dataset used exclusively for DSC’s background class? How would performance compare if baselines adopted this strategy?  
5. Discuss potential societal risks (e.g., surveillance misuse, bias amplification in rejection rates) arising from improved open set recognition, as ethical considerations are absent in the current manuscript.  

**Limitations:**  
The method’s strict dimensionality requirement forces reliance on DAM, which is computationally unsustainable for large *C* and underperforms in critical tasks like face verification. The authors acknowledge this but omit analysis of DAM’s failure modes or pathways for improvement. Additionally, the experimental comparisons are compromised by inconsistent training configurations and background data usage, casting doubt on the claimed superiority.  

**Rating:**  
**4: Borderline reject**  
While the geometric formulation of simplex-based classification is conceptually interesting and the open set results are promising, the work’s technical shortcomings—particularly scalability limitations, missing ablation studies, and experimental fairness issues—outweigh its strengths. The lack of thorough comparison with closely related methods ([1–3]) and insufficient motivation for dual-space optimization further diminish its contribution. Substantial revisions addressing these concerns are needed to justify acceptance.

reviews.summary:
Sorry for misunderstanding the paper. Revised review is here.

This paper proposed novel loss function for training deep neural network classifier, which can be generalizably applied to many domains such as evaluation for representation learning such as MoCo, BYOL or Masked Auto-Encoder. The loss function does not need any hyper-parameter tuning since the class centers are given as vertices of simplex and each data samples are trained to map to its corresponding class center. This way, traditional way of maximizing margin between samples from different classes and minimizing margin between samples from same class can be achieved in simple and effective way. While the limitation is the dimension of output feature of deep neural net should be larger or equal to C-1, authors mitigated the issue using DAM which can be further improved. In the experiment section, the authors gave intuition on how DSC clusters the data samples compared to other method, and how the learned representation can be semantically meaningful on unknown classes. The method achieved SOTA on most classification dataset which seems promising.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
strengths: 
1. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
2. The proposed method DSC generalizes to domains where the classification is required as a downstream task.
2. Proposed method achieves SOTA on various dataset

weaknesses:
1. More experiments on validating the proposed method seems required. For example, by getting learned representations from self-supervised pre-trained model (e.g., MoCo, BYOL, etc), the evaluation on downstream classification task with various loss functions will provide more strength on the proposed method. 
2. As authors pointed out in the contribution, experimental evidence on how this method is robust to unbalanced dataset needs to be addressed.
3. The solution of limitation seems not practical, since simple multi-layer perceptron is used between d-dimensional and C-1 dimensional vector which requires a lot of parameters of d and C-1 is large.
4. It would be better to show distance matrix of other baselines. Although baselines' distance between class centers from known classes exhibit difference, semantic property would be preserved. Reviewer is not sure if the learned representation with DSC is actually semantically meaningful since this is not preserved for known classes. Giving more explanations on this would be nice.

2. In equation (4), does the dimension of f_i, and s_{y_i} match? Since it seems that f_i is d-dimensional vector while s_{y_i} is C-dimensional vector. Please clarify this part.

This way, traditional way of maximizing margin between samples from different classes and minimizing margin between samples from same class can be achieved in simple and effective way. While the limitation is the dimension of output feature of deep neural net should be larger or equal to C-1, authors mitigated the issue using DAM which can be further improved. The method achieved SOTA on most classification dataset which seems promising.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
strengths: 
1. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
2. The proposed method DSC generalizes to domains where the classification is required as a downstream task.

None.

reviews.text:
## Hybrid Peer Review  

**Summary:**  
This paper introduces a novel loss function for training deep neural network classifiers that simultaneously maximizes margins in both Euclidean and angular spaces. The method enforces class samples to cluster around centers positioned at the vertices of a regular simplex inscribed in a hypersphere, ensuring equivalent pairwise distances between centers. This hyperparameter-free approach generalizes to domains requiring classification as a downstream task (e.g., evaluating representations from MoCo, BYOL, or Masked Auto-Encoders) and achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) results on most classification benchmarks. While the constraint that the feature dimension must be ≥ C-1 is mitigated by the Dimension Augmentation Module (DAM), the solution requires further refinement. Experiments demonstrate DSC’s effectiveness in clustering and generating semantically meaningful representations for unknown classes, though additional validation is needed.  

**Strengths and Weaknesses:**  
*Strengths:*  
1. **Well-structured and clear presentation**, with effective visualizations (e.g., embedding spaces).  
2. **Novel dual-space margin maximization**: The integration of simplex geometry into a loss function is innovative and grounded in high-dimensional theory.  
3. **Hyperparameter-free for closed-set tasks**, simplifying implementation and benefiting practitioners.  
4. **SOTA performance** in open-set recognition and competitive results in moderate-scale closed-set classification (e.g., CIFAR-100). The compact class regions enable reliable unknown-sample rejection.  

*Weaknesses:*  
1. **Limited experimental validation**:  
   - Self-supervised pre-trained models (e.g., MoCo, BYOL) should be evaluated with DSC on downstream tasks.  
   - Robustness to class imbalance (claimed in contributions) lacks empirical support.  
   - Testing in non-vision domains (e.g., NLP) is absent.  
2. **DAM scalability issues**:  
   - DAM underperforms in large-scale tasks (e.g., face verification), indicating inadequate feature augmentation. Its simple MLP design introduces significant parameters when *d* and *C* are large.  
   - Architectural details (e.g., layer dimensions, activations) are insufficient for reproducibility. Ablation studies are needed to diagnose failure modes.  
3. **Ambiguous semantic properties**:  
   - While DSC preserves semantics for *unknown* classes, within-class distance inconsistencies (e.g., Fig. 4) and unclear preservation for *known* classes require further explanation. Distance matrices for baselines should be included for comparison.  
4. **Unaddressed societal risks**: Improved open-set rejection could enable surveillance misuse or amplify biases against underrepresented groups.  

**Questions:**  
1. **Hypersphere radius (*u*)**: How was *u=64* chosen? Provide sensitivity analysis across datasets.  
2. **DAM limitations**: Why does DAM underperform despite resolving dimensionality? What computational overhead does it introduce?  
3. **Equation (4)**: Clarify dimensional consistency between feature vector *f_i* (d-dimensional) and simplex center *s_{y_i}* (C-dimensional).  
4. **Background-class loss (Eq. 5)**: Justify the heuristics *λ=1/(2×batch_size²)* and *m=u/2*. How sensitive is performance to these?  

**Limitations:**  
1. **Dimensionality constraint**: While DAM addresses *d < C-1*, it fails in large-scale settings (e.g., face verification). Theoretical guarantees for *C >> d* are unverified.  
2. **Societal impact**: Compact class regions could exacerbate bias in rejecting underrepresented groups. Deployment risks (e.g., surveillance) require ethical guidelines.  

**Recommendation:**  
**Rating: 5 (Borderline Accept)**  
This technically solid paper introduces an innovative loss function with strong open-set performance and practical value. However, reasons to accept (novelty, SOTA results) are tempered by:  
- Incomplete validation (self-supervised tasks, class imbalance, non-vision domains)  
- DAM’s scalability and reproducibility gaps  
- Unresolved questions about semantic preservation and societal risks  
The work warrants acceptance but requires major revisions to address these limitations.  

---  
**Hybrid Integration Notes:**  
- **Novelty**: Amplified main review’s strength using Alternative 1’s "dual-space margin maximization" and Alternative 2’s "simplex geometry" framing.  
- **DAM Weaknesses**: Combined main review’s parameter concern (Weakness #3) with Alternatives 2–4’s large-scale underperformance evidence.  
- **Semantic Meaning**: Augmented main review’s Weakness #4 with Alternative 4’s observation about Fig. 4 distance inconsistencies.  
- **Societal Impact**: Integrated ethical risks from Alternatives 1–4 into Limitations.  
- **Questions**: Merged main review’s technical queries (Q1–Q2) with Alternatives 2–4’s hypersphere (*u*) and DAM concerns.  
- **Decision**: Maintained "Borderline Accept" (Rating 5) but strengthened justification using weaknesses consolidated across all reviews.
reviews.original_text:
Summary:
Sorry for misunderstanding the paper. Revised review is here.

This paper proposed novel loss function for training deep neural network classifier, which can be generalizably applied to many domains such as evaluation for representation learning such as MoCo, BYOL or Masked Auto-Encoder. The loss function does not need any hyper-parameter tuning since the class centers are given as vertices of simplex and each data samples are trained to map to its corresponding class center. This way, traditional way of maximizing margin between samples from different classes and minimizing margin between samples from same class can be achieved in simple and effective way. While the limitation is the dimension of output feature of deep neural net should be larger or equal to C-1, authors mitigated the issue using DAM which can be further improved. In the experiment section, the authors gave intuition on how DSC clusters the data samples compared to other method, and how the learned representation can be semantically meaningful on unknown classes. The method achieved SOTA on most classification dataset which seems promising.

Strengths And Weaknesses:
strengths: 
1. The paper is well-written and easy to follow.
2. The proposed method DSC generalizes to domains where the classification is required as a downstream task.
2. Proposed method achieves SOTA on various dataset

weaknesses:
1. More experiments on validating the proposed method seems required. For example, by getting learned representations from self-supervised pre-trained model (e.g., MoCo, BYOL, etc), the evaluation on downstream classification task with various loss functions will provide more strength on the proposed method. 
2. As authors pointed out in the contribution, experimental evidence on how this method is robust to unbalanced dataset needs to be addressed.
3. The solution of limitation seems not practical, since simple multi-layer perceptron is used between d-dimensional and C-1 dimensional vector which requires a lot of parameters of d and C-1 is large.
4. It would be better to show distance matrix of other baselines. Although baselines' distance between class centers from known classes exhibit difference, semantic property would be preserved. Reviewer is not sure if the learned representation with DSC is actually semantically meaningful since this is not preserved for known classes. Giving more explanations on this would be nice.

Questions:
1. Does the length of the center vector u affect the performance of the DSC (or need tuning)? How does this value affect the performance?
2. In equation (4), does the dimension of f_i, and s_{y_i} match? Since it seems that f_i is d-dimensional vector while s_{y_i} is C-dimensional vector. Please clarify this part.

Limitations:
None.

Rating:
5: Borderline accept: Technically solid paper where reasons to accept outweigh reasons to reject, e.g., limited evaluation. Please use sparingly.
